title: Therapeutic Communication Techniques
subtitle: The nurse uses therapeutic communication — open-ended questions, reflection, silence, and clarifying — while avoiding blocks like false reassurance, giving advice, 'why' questions, and changing the subject.

patient.name: Therapeutic Communication
patient.admitDate: The nurse uses therapeutic communication to build trust and support the patient.

nursesNote: The nurse uses therapeutic communication to build trust and keep the interaction patient-centered. Helpful techniques include open-ended questions that invite the patient to share ('Tell me more about how you're feeling'), reflection that restates the feeling the patient expressed to show active listening, clarifying to confirm understanding ('What do you mean by that?'), purposeful silence that gives the patient time to think without being rushed, and offering self to convey presence ('I'll stay with you a while'). The nurse keeps the focus on the patient's feelings rather than on facts or the nurse's own opinions. Common communication blocks are avoided: false reassurance ('Everything will be fine') dismisses real concerns, giving advice ('If I were you...') removes the patient's autonomy, 'why' questions can sound accusing and put the patient on the defensive, and changing the subject signals that the topic is unwelcome and shuts down sharing. Closed-ended questions have a place for specific data but should not dominate an emotional conversation.
providerOrders: Use open-ended questions to invite the patient to share
Reflect and restate the patient's feelings to show listening
Use silence to give the patient time to process
Avoid false reassurance, giving advice, and 'why' questions
Keep the focus on the patient's feelings and concerns

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about therapeutic communication?

opts.text: Open-ended questions encourage the patient to share
Reflection shows the nurse is actively listening
Silence can give the patient time to process
False reassurance helps calm the patient's fears
Clarifying confirms the nurse understood correctly
The focus stays on the patient's feelings
Giving advice removes the patient's autonomy
'Why' questions can sound accusing
Changing the subject shuts down sharing
Offering self conveys presence
Saying 'Don't worry, it's fine' is therapeutic
Restating feelings encourages the patient to continue

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: An anxious patient says 'I'm scared about my surgery'

opts.text: Sit down and give the patient full attention
Reflect the feeling: 'You're feeling scared.'
Use an open question to explore the fear
Provide honest information and resources
Document the concern and follow up
'Tell me more about what's worrying you.'
'Don't worry, everything will be fine.'
'You sound frustrated about the delay.'
'If I were you, I'd just have the surgery.'
Sitting in silence while the patient gathers thoughts
'Why didn't you call the nurse sooner?'

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: A patient starts crying about a new diagnosis
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay present and acknowledge the emotion
Offer silence and a tissue, no rushing
Use an open question to let them express feelings
Offer support resources and answer questions honestly
Document the interaction
'What is on your mind right now?'
'Everyone feels that way; it's nothing.'
'It sounds like you feel overwhelmed.'
'Let's not talk about that right now.'
'I'll sit here with you for a few minutes.'
'You should just stay positive.'
'Can you help me understand what you mean?'
'Why would you feel like that?'

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Psychosocial Integrity, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
